smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: made these for you
thread.body_text:
hi Ellie, spent a few minutes on the Twisted Pepper site and I'm a little annoyed we're not working together yet haha.

but that's not why I reached out. I handle Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of sending a BS pitch, I built you some.

built 3 ad concepts from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and customer reviews. it's all done and waiting on my side.

should I send it over?

- Kyle

thread.subject: RE: made these for you
thread.body_text:
Ellie - the thing most brands notice first isn't the ads, it's the brand playbook. it maps your voice, the exact words your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's ready to go with the mockups.

 want me to pass both along?

- Kyle
please send me the brand playbook please

here you go Ellie - 3 ad mockups built from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus your brand playbook .

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Twisted Pepper is a founder-run gourmet dip, soup, rub and seasoning brand with great flavor stories but low prices and no ads running, so the whole call is about starting clean with gift bundles that clear the $40 free shipping bar.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A handcrafted gourmet food brand started by Ellie Hughes in 2005. Sells direct to consumer on Shopify and also does wholesale and fundraisers. Based in Wichita, Kansas.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: 103 products: dip mixes, soup mixes, BBQ rubs, seasonings, and gift boxes. Most single items are cheap, from $5.50 to about $13. The bundles and gift packs sit higher, around $22.99 to $36. Median price is $9.99, which we are using as a stand-in for their average order value since we cannot see their real numbers.
deep_dive.positioning: Approachable gourmet. Fine-restaurant flavor made fast and easy for busy home cooks. Gluten free, low sodium, no added MSG. Not mass-market bland.
deep_dive.who_buys: Busy home cooks who want bold flavor without the work, and gift-givers buying for people who love food. The gift boxes and multi-packs point straight at the gifting buyer.
customer_voice: I am absolutely addicted to your soups and dips/cheese ball mixes. To be honest - all those I bought from you never went for gifts! They went in our stomachs, and to share with extended family and friends over the holidays. They were a huge hit with everyone, so I will be restocking my hidden stash and sharing with friends.

Jalapeno Lime Rub is absolutely amazing. I use in avacado toast, deviled eggs, steak and chicken. Fantastic in a wrap using a low carb tortilla, spread the Laughing Cow Cheese, Sprinkle on Jalapeno Lime Rub, add turkey and it's a wrap.
My husband Loves the Pig Sweat Rub. He sprinkles on his bacon while cooking, and anything pork! I order 4 jars at time.
strengths: Strong brand voice and real flavor stories. Ellie's founder story is genuine and easy to build ads around.
They already have gift bundles and multi-packs priced at $22.99 to $36, which is the price point paid ads actually need.
gaps: Almost no visible reviews on the site. A few products show 3 to 7 reviews, most show none. Thin social proof makes cold traffic harder to convert.
Single products are far too cheap to advertise on their own. A $6 dip mix cannot carry ad cost. The whole plan has to push bundles and the $40 free shipping threshold.

game_plan.headline: Start from zero with the gift bundles, prove one winner, then scale it and add a second bundle.

phases.goal: Prove that cold traffic will buy at all, and find one ad and one product that works. No scale promises this month.
campaigns: One simple sales campaign with 2 or 3 ad sets, broad targeting, so Meta can find the buyers for us.
A basic retargeting ad set for people who visit the site but do not buy.
lead_products: Lead with the 4 Pack Dip Mixes at $22.99 (Dipping Madness or Veggies & Chiles). Why: it is priced high enough to survive ad cost, it is the kind of thing people share at parties and holidays, and the review we have says a customer 'orders 4 jars at a time' and shares with family. Single $6 dips cannot carry ads, bundles can.
Test the Spiced Up 3 Pack Seasonings Collection at $29 as the gift angle. Why: it clears the $40 free shipping bar with one more item and speaks straight to gift-givers.
phases.creative: Run the 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet we already sent. Go live first with the ad built around the 4 Pack Dip Mixes, because it is the most giftable and the highest ticket, so it has the best shot at paying for itself. The founder story ad goes second. The single-flavor hero ad goes third.
phases.budget: Start at $50 to $75 a day across the whole account.
phases.what_success_looks_like: We see which ad gets cheap clicks and at least a few sales. We are looking for one clear winner to put money behind, not profit yet.

phases.goal: Put more money behind whatever won in month one and widen who sees it.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad set and raise budget in steps, not all at once.
Add 1 or 2 new audiences to widen reach beyond the first winners.
Keep retargeting running and add a small email capture push tied to their existing signup offer.
lead_products: Keep pushing whichever bundle won in Phase 1.
Lean harder on the $40 free shipping plus free gift offer to lift order size, since single items are cheap.
phases.creative: Make 2 or 3 fresh versions of the winning ad. Same idea, new hooks. Start using real customer quotes in the copy to fix the thin social proof problem.
phases.budget: Raise to $100 to $150 a day only if month one showed a real winner.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad holds up at higher spend and order size creeps up as more people hit the free shipping bar.

phases.goal: Scale the proven play and add a second product line so we are not resting on one bundle.
campaigns: Launch a second product angle: the Soup Collection 3 packs at $30, or a BBQ rub bundle heading into their BBQ season.
Build a repeat-buyer push over email for people who already bought once.
lead_products: Home Cookin' or Comfort Soup Collection 3 pack at $30. Why: same high-enough price as the dip packs, strong gift and cozy-season appeal, and soups were called out by the addicted customer review.
Uptown BBQ Rub or a rub bundle if the call happens near grilling season, since the homepage itself leads with 'It's BBQ Time'.
phases.creative: Roll the winning format onto the new product. Keep refreshing the top ad so it does not go stale.
phases.budget: Scale in steps toward $200+ a day only if the return holds.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two products selling from ads, a growing email list, and repeat orders starting to show up.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: enough to get roughly 30 to 50 clicks a day at typical food-brand click costs, which is the floor for learning which ad and product works before spending more

estimates.label: Order size we are working toward
estimates.basis: their median single item is $9.99, but the bundles we are leading with run $22.99 to $36, and the $40 free shipping offer nudges people to add one more item

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: we will not put a dollar figure on this until we see month one
estimates.basis: we have no access to their sales, conversion rate, or margin, so any 90-day revenue number now would be a guess. We need the discovery answers first.

assumptions: Their margin on the $22.99 to $36 bundles is healthy enough to pay for ads. If the margin is thin, the whole plan changes.
They can ship reliably and fast, since the site claims fast shipping.
They have never run Meta ads before, so we are starting from zero, not fixing a broken account.
The gift bundles are the right lead product. If their real bestseller is something else, we lead with that instead.

questions.question: Have you ever run Meta or Facebook ads before, even a boosted post?
questions.listening_for: We found no ads running. Confirm whether they truly start from zero or tried and stopped. If they stopped, why matters a lot.

questions.question: Who handles your marketing today, is it just you or do you have help?
questions.listening_for: This is a founder-run brand. Listen for whether Ellie does everything herself and how much time she has. It tells us how hands-on we need to be.

questions.question: What does it cost you to make and ship one of the $22.99 four packs, roughly?
questions.listening_for: We cannot see margin. This decides if paid ads can even work. If most of the $22.99 is cost, ads will lose money and we should say so.

questions.question: What kind of monthly ad budget feels comfortable to test with?
questions.listening_for: Whether $50 to $75 a day is realistic for them. If they can only do $10 a day, the plan will not get enough data to work.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site cold, not a friend or a market customer, how often do they actually buy?
questions.listening_for: A sense of whether the site converts strangers at all. Cold ad traffic is very different from repeat market fans.

questions.question: What is your most popular product and your biggest order size right now?
questions.listening_for: This tells us if bundles really are the right lead. If a single item outsells everything, we rethink the lead product. Also confirms real average order size.

questions.question: I noticed almost no reviews show on your product pages. Do you collect reviews anywhere, like email or in person?
questions.listening_for: Thin social proof is a real risk. If they have reviews sitting somewhere unused, we can put them on the site and in ads fast.

questions.question: Does most of your revenue come from online, wholesale, or in-person markets and events?
questions.listening_for: If they mostly sell in person, the website may not be built to convert cold traffic, and we would need to fix that before scaling spend.

questions.question: Do people ever tell you they found you by searching Google for things like gluten free dip mix or dry soup mix?
questions.listening_for: People do search for terms like 'gluten free dip mix' and 'dry soup mix.' That is a real Google opening. Listen for whether they show up or are invisible there.

questions.question: Do you get repeat customers reordering the same flavors, and do you email them?
questions.listening_for: The addicted-customer review suggests strong repeat buyers. If they are not emailing them, there is easy repeat revenue to capture without more ad spend.

objections.objection: My products are only $6 to $10, ads will never pay off.
objections.answer: You are right that a $6 dip cannot carry an ad. That is exactly why we lead with your $22.99 four packs and $29 to $36 gift collections, and push your own $40 free shipping offer so people add more. We are advertising the basket, not the cheapest item.

objections.objection: I have never run ads and I do not want to waste money.
objections.answer: That is why month one is a small test at $50 to $75 a day. The only goal is to find one ad and one product that works before we spend more. We do not scale until something proves itself.

objections.objection: I do most of my sales at markets and wholesale, not online.
objections.answer: Good to know, and that changes where we start. If the website is not set up to convert strangers yet, we fix that first so we are not paying to send people to a page that leaks. We will get honest about that on this call.

objections.objection: My reviews are mostly word of mouth, they are not on the site.
objections.answer: That is actually an opportunity. You clearly have happy repeat buyers, we saw quotes like the customer who orders four jars at a time. We just need to get those reviews onto your pages and into the ads so strangers trust you too.

red_flags.flag: Single products are very cheap, median $9.99, and many are $5.50 to $9.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Paid ads cannot make money on a $6 item. The plan only works if we lead with bundles and the $40 free shipping bar. If their real orders are mostly single cheap items, the economics may not hold.

red_flags.flag: Almost no reviews visible on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold shoppers need proof before buying food they have never tasted. With little social proof, conversion will be harder until we surface real reviews.

red_flags.flag: The shipping policy page says 'Coming Soon' with no real content.
red_flags.why_it_matters: A missing shipping policy hurts trust and can raise support questions at checkout. Worth flagging as an easy fix before we send paid traffic.

red_flags.flag: We have zero access to their real numbers.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot confirm margin, conversion, or current revenue. Kyle must not quote any figure as fact. Every number on the call is a public-based estimate only.

other_channel_openings: Google Search is a real opening. People actively search for terms like 'gluten free dip mix,' 'dry soup mix,' and 'BBQ rub.' Worth exploring once Meta is proven, since these are known products people look for by name.
SEO on their recipe pages is a soft opening. They already publish recipes like Spinach Artichoke Hot Dip and Lentil Soup, which people search for and which naturally tie to their products.
